titleStmt.title: The Collected Letters of Robert Southey. Part Four: 1810-1815
A Romantic Circles Electronic Edition

publicationStmt.publisher: Romantic Circles, http://www.rc.umd.edu, University of Maryland

availability.p: Material from the Romantic Circles Website may not be downloaded, reproduced or disseminated in any manner without authorization unless it is for purposes of criticism, comment, news reporting, teaching, and/or classroom use as provided by the Copyright Act of 1976, as amended.

Unless otherwise noted, all Pages and Resources mounted on Romantic Circles are copyrighted by the author/editor and may be shared only in accordance with the Fair Use provisions of U.S. copyright law. Except as expressly permitted by this statement, redistribution or republication in any medium requires express prior written consent from the author/editors and advance notification of Romantic Circles. Any requests for authorization should be forwarded to Romantic Circles:> Romantic Circles c/o Professor Neil Fraistat Department of English University of Maryland College Park, MD 20742 fraistat@umd.edu

By their use of these texts and images, users agree to the following conditions:
These texts and images may not be used for any commercial purpose without prior written permission from Romantic Circles.
These texts and images may not be re-distributed in any forms other than their current ones.

Users are not permitted to download these texts and images in order to mount them on their own servers. It is not in our interest or that of our users to have uncontrolled subsets of our holdings available elsewhere on the Internet. We make corrections and additions to our edited resources on a continual basis, and we want the most current text to be the only one generally available to all Internet users. Institutions can, of course, make a link to the copies at Romantic Circles, subject to our conditions of use.

analytic.title: 1873. Robert Southey to Thomas Woodruffe Smith, 20 February 1811

monogr.title: The Collected Letters of Robert Southey, Part Four
Electronic Editions

imprint.publisher: Romantic Circles, http://www.rc.umd.edu, University of Maryland

sourceDesc.p: Morgan Library, MA 4500. ALS; 2p.. Not previously published.
These letters were edited with the assistance of Ian Packer and Lynda Pratt

quotation.p: All quotation marks and apostrophes have been changed: " for “," for ”, ' for ‘, and ' for ’.

hyphenation.p: Any dashes occurring in line breaks have been removed.
Because of web browser variability, all hyphens have been typed on the U.S. keyboard.
Dashes have been rendered as a variable number of hyphens to give a more exact rendering of their length.

normalization.p: Southey's spelling has not been regularized.
Writing in other hands appearing on these manuscripts has been indicated as such, the content recorded in brackets.
& has been used for the ampersand sign.
£ has been used for £, the pound sign
All other characters, those with accents, non-breaking spaces, etc., have been encoded in HTML entity decimals.

taxonomy.bibl: NINES categories for Genre and Material Form at http://www.performantsoftware.com/nines_wiki/index.php/Submitting_RDF#.3Cnines:genre.3E on 2009-02-26

Ran Transforms for proof copies of Southey Part 4

div.head:
1873. Robert Southey to Thomas Woodruffe Smith, 20 February 1811Address: To/ T. W. Smith Esqr/ Stockwell Park./ Surry
Stamped: KESWICK/ 298
Postmarks: E/ 23FE23/ 1811; [partial] 10o’Clock/ FE.23/ 1811
Seal: Black wax, with ‘S’ and motto ‘In Labor Quies’ below
Endorsement: Robert Southey/ late Poet Laureat
MS: The surviving manuscript is now split between two archives. The opening page (‘Keswick. Feby. 20. 1811’ to ‘incessantly making for’) is in the archive at Westminster School; AL, 2p. The remainder of the letter, including the address leaf is in the Morgan Library, MA 4500. ALS; 2p.
Unpublished.
Dating note: dating from postmark.
div.p: I have delayed thanking you for your last letter for many days past, in expectation of receiving the particulars of a case upon which I intended to apply to you, – knowing no other person so likely to possess that power of doing good in such cases, which is obtained by habits of conspicuous liberality.

The object is to dispose of four miserable children who will soon be orphans, & are at present worse than orphans. I have an intimate friend at Bristol, by name Charles Danvers whom I believe you once saw there, a most excellent & exemplary man. He was the second of three brothers, the eldest of whom (now dead)Daniel Danvers (d. 1807), a Captain in the Royal Marines. squandered in his youth the main property of the family; & the youngestJohn Danvers (d. 1812), a surgeon and apothecary then living in Woolwich, was declared bankrupt in 1808. is the father of the children in question. This person was bred to physic, & well established in his profession in the neighbourhood of Bristol, – besides which he <had> some little patrimony xx coming to him on his mothers death, – from 1500 to 2000£. He fell into sottish habits, & married a woman Unidentified beyond the information given here. whom his example & the miseries which it soon began to bring on, partly seduced & partly drove to the same practices. Of course he soon became a ruined man, – & the whole of his money was swallowed up during his mothers life. An appointment in the army was obtained for him;John Danvers was appointed an Assistant Surgeon in the Royal Marines in 1805 and was put on half pay in 1809. – this too he lost by gross & incurable misconduct. His brother Charles paying debt for him after debt, obtaining situations for him each humbler than the last, – only to hear that in a few weeks <days> he had forfeited what it had been the anxious labour of weeks to procure for him, – rescuing his very bed from the sheriffs officer,A bailiff who was enforcing a court order to recover debts. & redeeming him from prison, – has gone to the very utmost of his own means, even to distressing himself, without any other advantage than the consolation of having spared no effort to save him. The mother is happily dead, – the father at this time in S t George’s Hospital, St George’s Hospital, founded in 1733 in Lanesborough House at Hyde Park Corner. in a state of incurable debility both of body & mind, the mere effect of drink. He will never come out of the hospital alive unless he is discharged as incurable, – & they will hardly turn <him> out to die.

The children are now placed with a woman who takes good care of them, & has made them clean – probably for the first time in their lives. Their father is past the power of giving any opinion about them, – they are on Charles’s hands, – & he, reduced as he is by the efforts which he has now been incessantly making for eight years, is unable to bear this farther burthen. See also Southey to Charles Danvers, 10 January 1811, Letter 1851. He wishes therefore to place them in some charity school. I fear the eldest is too old – It is for the account of their ages that I have been waiting, & it has reached me this evening.

Now my dear Sir I have laid this dismal story before you without preamble. Beneficence is power Wealth we have often been told is power, – but Beneficence is power also. Among the many charitable institutions to which you have so liberally contributed, is there any to which you could consign these poor children? Should any farther enquiry concerning them be needful, any questions will be answered, or any trouble undertaken by Mr Thomas Reid, Possibly Thomas Whitehead Reid (1786–1845), younger half-brother of Samuel Reid. Originally a sugar-refiner, he was by this time a merchant in London. No 4. Leman Street, Goodman’s Fields. – It is needless to apologize for having thus applied to you. If you can serve them I am sure you will be glad of the opportunity of doing good.

I read with great concern the account of your sufferings from your daughters Ann Woodruffe Smith (d. 1822). In 1811 she married John Barton (1789–1852; DNB), political economist and botanist, and half-brother of the poet, Bernard Barton. pen, & also in a later letter from our friend Duppa. In May I shall hope to see you, – more frequently too than on my former visits to London, as my head quarters are to be at Streatham. We have had a sickly winter; – the season has been more variable than any within my memory; & one or other of the children has constantly been ailing, – sometimes all four at once. This has much harassed me at times, but I continue in good health, God be thanked, & working the harder to win leisure for a journey in the spring. There is an article of mine in the last Quarterly Review upon the Evangelical Sects, Southey’s review of Hints to the Public and the Legislature, on the Nature and Effect of Evangelical Preaching. By a Barrister (1809), in Quarterly Review, 4 (November 1810), 480–514. – the general principles of which I think would accord with yours, – as I am sure the spirit would <will> in which it is written.

closer.salute: Believe my dear Sir yours with true regard & respect
